title: Dog Bunny Hopping While Running: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog bunny hopping while running? Learn about hip dysplasia, gait changes, and when to see a vet in this empathetic triage guide.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bunny hopping is a compensatory gait used by dogs to reduce hip or back pain, typically indicating a chronic orthopedic issue that requires a scheduled veterinary exam rather than emergency care.

What does it mean when my dog is bunny hopping?

Bunny hopping is a specific gait where a dog moves both hind legs together simultaneously, rather than in an alternating pattern, while running or climbing stairs. While it might look cute or quirky, it is often a compensatory movement used by dogs to minimize discomfort or instability in their hips or lower back. By moving both legs together, they shift the weight-bearing burden and reduce the range of motion required in individual joints.

Is my dog's bunny hopping a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for bunny hopping is generally Low if your dog is otherwise eating, drinking, and able to walk.
  • It is usually a sign of a chronic orthopedic condition, such as hip dysplasia or luxating patellas, rather than an immediate emergency.
  • Schedule a routine veterinary appointment for a physical exam and potential X-rays to address the clinical signs.

How can providing video and photos help my veterinarian triage the issue?

  • Capture a video of your dog running in their natural environment to prevent clinic adrenaline from masking the gait abnormality.
  • Record clear footage at eye level from the side and from behind to show the mechanics of the hop.
  • Provide these visuals to help your vet differentiate between hip, knee, or spinal issues before the physical exam begins.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A "bunny-hopping" gait in dogs, characterized by the simultaneous movement of both hindlimbs, can be indicative of several underlying conditions. Hip dysplasia may present with a bunny-hopping gait, especially after exercise, along with lameness, joint laxity, reduced range of motion, crepitation, and pain during full extension and flexion. In puppies, a bunny-hopping gait with weak or absent spinal reflexes can be a sign of parasitic infection with Toxoplasma gondii or Neospora caninum, typically developing between 3-8 weeks of age. This can progress to severe extensor rigidity, muscle atrophy, and potentially fatal respiratory paralysis. Serum creatine kinase (CK) concentration is often increased in such cases.

Chapter: Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1197)

faqs.question: Does bunny hopping always mean hip dysplasia?
faqs.answer: While bunny hopping is a classic sign of hip dysplasia, it can also be caused by patellar luxation, spinal issues, or even temporary muscle strain in younger dogs.

faqs.question: At what age do dogs start bunny hopping?
faqs.answer: It can be seen in puppies as young as a few months old if they have congenital hip issues, but it may also develop later in life as arthritis or joint degredation progresses.

faqs.question: Should I limit my dog's exercise if I see them bunny hopping?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid high-impact activities like jumping or long-distance running until a veterinarian has evaluated the cause, as excessive strain can worsen joint laxity.
